title: Today's Tech Brief: Zig creator seeks 'uncompromising perfection' before blessing 1.0
summary: A look at the latest tech news, including Zig's quest for perfection and the value of domain expertise.

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's tech brief. I'm always on the lookout for interesting developments in the tech world, and I've got a couple of items that caught my eye recently.

First up, the creator of the Zig programming language, Andrew Kelley, is taking a stand on quality. In a recent interview, he mentioned that he's holding out for 'uncompromising perfection' before he considers releasing version 1.0 of Zig. This approach might seem old-school, but it's interesting to see someone prioritizing quality in a world where rapid iteration and deployment are often the norm. I mean, who needs speed when you can have perfection, right?

Kelley also had some thoughts on AI-powered coding tools, calling paying monthly for cloud-powered AI coding an 'insane proposition'. I have to agree - there's something to be said for keeping control of your codebase. It's like having your own kitchen instead of ordering takeout all the time. You might get used to the convenience, but you lose control over the recipe.

Zig creator seeks 'uncompromising perfection' before blessing 1.0 is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: Software Zig creator seeks 'uncompromising perfection' before blessing 1.0 Andrew Kelley interview describes paying monthly for cloud-powered AI coding as an 'insane proposition' Tim Anderson Tim Anderson Published thu 28 May 2026 // 16:00 UTC Andrew Kelley, inventor and BDFL (benevolent dictator for life) of the Zig programming language, was interviewed by... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

Domain expertise has always been the real moat is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: May 30, 2026 Software Engineering AI Domain Expertise Has Always Been the Real Moat The hard part of writing software has never been the writing. It was building a working model of the domain in your head first. Before you could ship a payroll system you had to understand garnishments and pre-tax deductions and what happens when someone’s pay period straddl... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
